ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKN represents raw beef meat broadly; no specific ribeye lean-trimmed code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Adapted from the closest defensible parent category.

preparation_method: trimmed of external and internal separable fat

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw lean ribeye steak portion is approximately 150g, representing a single restaurant-style or home-cooked serving before cooking losses are applied.

Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef rib eye steak, separable lean only, raw. Values are consistent between both sources. HPB values used as primary given Singapore provenance of the input record.

gi_source.title: N/A — Beef ribeye steak, raw, lean contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food. Carbohydrate content is 0g/100g (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central). Pure protein and fat foods do not elicit a meaningful glycemic response and are not assigned a GI value. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index field set to numeric 0 to satisfy template numeric requirement; gi_category is not_applicable to convey clinical inapplicability.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (57mg/100g) and high potassium (380mg/100g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio supportive of blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Lean beef ribeye is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades; the natural sodium content of 57mg/100g is well within DASH diet limits, and the potassium content of 380mg/100g actively supports vasodilation and blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein (23.8g/100g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability without raising postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lean beef ribeye has no glycaemic impact due to zero carbohydrate content and is an appropriate protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; limit portion to 90–120g cooked and pair with non-starchy vegetables, avoiding sugar-based glazes or sauces that would add carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.1g/100g) and cholesterol (58mg/100g) are present but low for a lean-trimmed cut; regular red meat consumption is associated with modest cardiovascular risk elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean-trimmed beef ribeye is acceptable in a heart-healthy diet when consumed in moderation (no more than 2–3 servings per week of 90–120g cooked); the low saturated fat content of 1.1g/100g is significantly lower than untrimmed ribeye, but individuals with established CVD should prioritise fish, legumes, and poultry as primary protein sources and monitor total red meat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid which can drive pro-inflammatory cytokine production relevant to RA; absence of omega-3, antioxidants, or fibre provides no offsetting anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including lean beef ribeye to no more than 1–2 servings per week and preferentially substitute with oily fish (salmon, sardines) or plant-based proteins to reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef ribeye steak, raw and lean, is a premium cut of beef taken from the rib section (ribs 6–12) of the cattle carcass, with all separable external and internal visible fat removed, leaving only the lean muscle meat. In its raw state it displays a deep red colour with fine intramuscular marbling that contributes to its characteristic flavour and tenderness even after fat trimming. The lean-only preparation significantly reduces total fat to 2.8g/100g and saturated fat to approximately 1.1g/100g compared to untrimmed ribeye, while preserving the high protein content of 23.8g/100g. Energy density is low at 121 kcal/100g, making it one of the leaner red meat options available. The cut is naturally free of carbohydrates and sugar, producing no glycaemic response, which makes it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is naturally low at 57mg/100g and potassium is high at 380mg/100g, supporting cardiovascular and blood pressure health when prepared without added salt. Ribeye is rich in haem iron (2.2mg/100g), zinc (approximately 4.5mg/100g), vitamin B12 (approximately 2.5mcg/100g), and selenium, making it nutritionally valuable for iron-deficiency prevention and neurological health. It is widely consumed across Western cuisines as a grilled, pan-seared, or oven-roasted steak. In Singapore and Southeast Asia it appears in Western-style restaurants and steakhouses. Clinically, lean beef ribeye is appropriate for diabetic and hypertensive individuals in controlled portions but should be consumed in moderation by those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its red meat classification and arachidonic acid content.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database with confidence 0.95 for this exact food item (Beef ribeye steak, raw, lean), cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef rib eye steak separable lean only raw. Both sources are approved primary sources and values are consistent. Extended nutrient fields (fatty acid profile, B-vitamins, minerals) estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for the same food category where HPB does not report them.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw lean beef ribeye — this food contains no carbohydrates by composition (naturally zero). Sugar_g is naturally zero as beef muscle meat contains no free sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle food with no plant fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_index field is set to numeric 0 (the template-required numeric type) with gi_category not_applicable to convey clinical inapplicability; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: lean beef ribeye contains negligible preformed vitamin A in muscle meat; detailed RAE data not consistently reported in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this specific lean-trimmed cut. Vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: vitamin D content of lean beef muscle is negligible and not consistently reported across approved sources for this cut. Vitamin_e_mg set to -1: vitamin E data for lean beef ribeye not available in HPB database; USDA SR Legacy reports trace amounts below reliable detection for lean-only cuts. Trans_fat_g value of 0.1g/100g reflects naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and vaccenic acid present in ruminant beef fat; this is a naturally occurring trans fat distinct from industrial trans fats. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and extended B-vitamin and mineral values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13349 (Beef, rib eye steak/roast, separable lean only, raw) where HPB does not report these fields. The input record's rheumatoid_arthritis caution referencing 'Trans fat 0.1g/100g — strongly pro-inflammatory at any amount' has been contextualised: the trans fat present is naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (CLA/vaccenic acid), not industrial trans fat. Halal status assumed based on input dietary_tags; kosher status not confirmed and set to false. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from input retained in meal_context but noted as atypical for raw steak; more typical meal contexts (dinner, main course) added.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Beef ribeye steak, raw, lean (F06030012) passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The iteration 2 failure on q4 was due to glycemic_index being set to null instead of a numeric value. This has been corrected to numeric 0 with gi_category not_applicable, satisfying both the template numeric requirement and the clinical inapplicability of GI for zero-carbohydrate foods. Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore (primary, confidence 0.95) cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Zero carbohydrate, sugar, and fibre values are justified as naturally zero for animal muscle meat. All three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are real and defensible. Western cuisine classification does not trigger mandatory non-English language requirements. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent.
